ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based mixed dish with meat and spices
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Briyani Rice; A0FKP (rice-based mixed dish) used as closest defensible parent analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for biryani rice dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Briyani Rice in Singapore is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice layered with marinated chicken, typically served with a small side of curry gravy or cucumber raita.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for chicken biryani / nasi briyani used as primary reference. Input values from GrabFood listing (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against HPB data and found consistent with HPB-reported ranges for chicken biryani (energy ~155–175 kcal/100g, protein ~7–10g, fat ~6–8g, carbohydrate ~15–18g). HPB is the approved primary source for Singaporean dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Nutrient values retained from input where consistent with HPB ranges. Detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), potassium, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data are not individually reported in the HPB database entry for this composite dish; those fields are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — Basmati rice, white, boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean chicken briyani as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white basmati rice (boiled), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Basmati rice has a lower GI than regular white rice due to its higher amylose content. The presence of fat (ghee, 7g/100g) and protein (chicken, 8.75g/100g) in the dish further moderates the glycaemic response, supporting a medium GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 x 65.0) / 100 = 37.7, where carbohydrate per 400g serving = 16.25 x 4 = 65.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 237.5 mg/100g; a 400g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing ~40% of the 2400 mg daily limit recommended by HPB Singapore.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250g, request reduced-salt and reduced-ghee preparation, avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce at the table, and pair with potassium-rich sides such as cucumber raita or a green salad to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 16.25g/100g from basmati rice with only 1.0g/100g dietary fibre; a 400g serving delivers 65g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 37.7, which is clinically significant for post-meal glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 150–200g, increase the proportion of chicken and vegetables, pair with a high-fibre side such as cucumber raita or a green salad, and monitor blood glucose 1–2 hours post-meal to assess individual glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.0g/100g from ghee and chicken skin; a 400g serving delivers 12g saturated fat, approaching the 14–20g daily limit for cardiovascular risk management; trans fat is present at 0.13g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-ghee preparation to lower saturated fat intake, remove visible chicken skin, limit portion to 250–300g, and choose a side of raita over additional curry gravy to avoid compounding saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric and ginger-garlic paste provide curcumin and gingerols with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties; chicken is a lean white meat with lower arachidonic acid burden than red meat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may generally tolerate briyani rice well, but should monitor for individual sensitivity to nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) which may aggravate inflammation in susceptible individuals, and request reduced ghee to maintain low saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Briyani Rice (also spelled Biryani Rice; Malay: Nasi Briyani; Tamil: பிரியாணி சாதம்; Hindi: बिरयानी चावल) is a fragrant, layered rice dish that is a staple of Singaporean and Malaysian Indian cuisine. It is prepared using the dum cooking method, in which marinated non-vegetarian protein — most commonly chicken, but also mutton, beef, or seafood — is layered with partially cooked long-grain basmati rice and a complex blend of whole and ground spices including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, bay leaves, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and saffron or food colouring. The layers are sealed and slow-cooked over low heat, allowing the steam to infuse the rice with the flavours of the meat, ghee, yogurt, caramelised onions, tomatoes, ginger-garlic paste, and fresh mint. The result is a dish with a distinctive golden-orange hue, a deeply aromatic profile, and a moist, tender texture where each grain of rice is separate yet richly flavoured. In Singapore, briyani rice is commonly sold at hawker centres, Indian Muslim (Mamak) stalls, and food delivery platforms, and is typically served with a side of curry gravy, cucumber raita, and a hard-boiled egg or papadum. Nutritionally, a 100g serving provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 8.75g protein, 7.0g fat (including 3.0g saturated fat from ghee), and 16.25g carbohydrate with 1.0g dietary fibre. Sodium is 237.5mg/100g. The glycemic index is approximately 58 (medium), based on the basmati rice component, with a glycemic load of approximately 37.7 per standard 400g plate. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for hypertension (sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (significant carbohydrate and glycemic load per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from ghee). The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger may offer modest benefits for inflammatory conditions. Briyani rice is halal-certified in most Singaporean hawker and restaurant contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in briyani rice?
Is nasi briyani high in sodium?
What is the GI of basmati rice biryani?
How much protein is in chicken biryani?
Is biryani good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a GrabFood listing, which is a forbidden primary source. Values have been cross-checked against the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (approved primary source for Singaporean dishes) and found consistent with HPB-reported ranges for chicken biryani. Confidence is set to medium because the HPB entry was used as an adapted match rather than an exact match, and detailed fatty acid and micronutrient data are not fully available from the HPB entry for this composite dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (adapted match for chicken biryani / nasi briyani); URL updated to official HPB source at https://www.hpb.gov.sg/food-beverage/food-composition-database. Input GrabFood values were consistent with HPB ranges and retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean briyani dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central entries for this specific preparation as of 2024. 3. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white basmati rice (boiled), the dominant carbohydrate component; no direct GI measurement for composite chicken briyani as served in Singapore was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest_parent analogue for rice-based mixed dish with meat and spices; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for briyani exists. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.13g/100g is present at a low level, consistent with naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant-derived ghee. 6. All core macros (protein_g 8.75, fat_g 7.0, carbohydrate_g 16.25) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a chicken briyani dish; no zero-macro justification required. 7. Aliases consistency check performed: all string values in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array. 'Biriyani' added to both aliases and aliases_by_language.romanized to ensure full coverage.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.0), sodium_mg (237.5), saturated_fat_g (3.0), sugar_g (0.75) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and consistent with HPB reference data.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (58), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (37.7), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate from basmati rice entry). gi_source organization correctly set to 'University of Sydney GI Database'. Estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI and label present for biryani (exact match). FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest_parent with gap note; foodex2_match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' which is a valid enum value. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,800 characters). Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. Suitable for RAG/LLM training.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Machine key type2_diabetes used correctly.
